passages.title: The Lantern List
passages.subject: ELA
passages.text:
Nia carried a lantern list in her jacket pocket on Friday morning. Grandma called it a lantern list, but Nia called it a promise. The neighborhood festival was only one day away, and Nia wanted the library steps to shine brighter than they had the year before.

By lunchtime, Nia had written twelve jobs beside her name. She would paint the paper stars, hang the lantern string, sweep the steps, and test every tiny bulb. Mateo read the list and raised his eyebrows. "That is a parade for one person," he said. Nia folded the paper and said she could do it.

After school, the first blue lantern slipped from Nia's hand and rolled under a bench. The ladder wobbled when she reached for the string. Mateo held the ladder while Nia tied the first blue lantern. Then Grandma brought a box of red paper stars, and two younger neighbors asked what job they could do.

Nia looked at the lantern list again. The promise had started to feel heavy, like a backpack full of stones. She crossed out half of her own jobs and added names beside them. Mateo tested the bulbs. Grandma taped the stars. The younger neighbors swept the steps in careful rows.

At dusk, every lantern swung safely above the library steps. Nia noticed that the lights did not look smaller because she had shared the work. They looked brighter. When Mateo asked what she would write on next year's lantern list, Nia smiled and wrote the first job in big letters: ask the helpers early.

passages.title: The Porch Bell
passages.subject: ELA
passages.text:
Milo heard the porch bell before he saw the package. The bell was small and brass, tied to the rail with a faded green ribbon. Whenever the wind pushed the screen door, the porch bell made a bright ding that meant someone had arrived.

The package held a new sketchbook from Aunt Rosa. Milo wanted to keep it clean until he could draw something perfect. His little sister Tessa leaned over the porch step and asked for one page. Milo hugged the sketchbook to his chest and said the first drawing had to be special.

That afternoon, the porch bell rang again. Tessa had dropped a basket of clothespins, and the wind was carrying them across the yard. Milo almost stayed on the step with his clean sketchbook. Then he saw Tessa chasing pins near the rose bushes, so he set the book down and ran to help.

One clothespin landed beside the porch bell. Another caught on the green ribbon. Milo and Tessa laughed as they gathered the last pins into the basket. A smudge of dirt crossed Milo's thumb, and the thumbprint landed on the first blank page.

Milo stared at the mark. Then he drew the porch rail around it, the green ribbon above it, and Tessa holding the basket below it. The smudge became a tiny stone on the path. When Aunt Rosa called that evening, Milo said the first page was special after all because it showed the day the porch bell reminded him to help.

choices.rationale: This states the message and uses the lantern result.

choices.rationale: Nia changes the list instead of hiding it.

choices.rationale: This names a helper but not the lesson by itself.

partB.rationale: Together these details show Nia sharing work and reaching a successful result.

items.prompt: Put the story events in the order that shows how Nia learns the message.
items.instructionText: Drag each event into the correct order.

tokens.text: Nia crosses out jobs and adds helpers' names.
tokens.evidenceQuote: She crossed out half of her own jobs and added names beside them.

tokens.text: Nia writes twelve jobs beside her own name.
tokens.evidenceQuote: By lunchtime, Nia had written twelve jobs beside her name.

tokens.text: Every lantern swings safely above the steps.
tokens.evidenceQuote: At dusk, every lantern swung safely above the library steps.

partialCreditRules.rule: All required tokens placed in the supported target or order position.
Two required tokens placed correctly without exceeding target capacity.
One required token placed correctly without exceeding target capacity.
Wrong placement, unanswered required placement, extra distractor placement, or capacity exceeded.

scoring.scoringNotes: Full credit requires every required token and no wrong target, presented-order shortcut, or capacity violation.

items.subject: ELA
items.prompt: Put the story events in the order that shows Milo learning the lesson.
items.instructionText: Drag each event into the correct order.

tokens.text: Milo draws the porch rail around the thumbprint.
tokens.evidenceQuote: Then he drew the porch rail around it, the green ribbon above it, and Tessa holding the basket below it.

tokens.text: Milo wants to keep the sketchbook clean.
tokens.evidenceQuote: Milo wanted to keep it clean until he could draw something perfect.

tokens.text: Milo sets the book down and runs to help.
tokens.evidenceQuote: Then he saw Tessa chasing pins near the rose bushes, so he set the book down and ran to help.

items.subject: ELA
items.stem: How does Nia change in the story? Use two details from the passage to support your answer.
items.instructionText: Write a short answer that explains how Nia's actions change and supports the answer with two passage details.
items.expectedAnswerCore: Nia changes from trying to do every lantern job herself to sharing the work with helpers.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: By lunchtime, Nia had written twelve jobs beside her name.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: Nia first writes twelve jobs beside her own name.
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This shows she begins by trying to handle the lantern work alone.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: She crossed out half of her own jobs and added names beside them.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: Nia later crosses out some of her jobs and adds helpers' names.
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This shows she changes her actions by sharing the work.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: Nia noticed that the lights did not look smaller because she had shared the work.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: Nia sees that sharing the work makes the lanterns look brighter.
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This shows she understands the value of her changed actions.

rubric.points3: A 3-point response states that Nia changes from trying to do every lantern job herself to sharing the work with helpers. It uses at least two accurate details from The Lantern List.
rubric.points2: A 2-point response gives a partial explanation of Nia's change and includes at least one accurate passage detail from The Lantern List.
rubric.points1: A 1-point response gives a weak or incomplete idea about Nia, gives no useful text support, or is mostly copied passage text from The Lantern List.
rubric.points0: A 0-point response is unrelated to The Lantern List, inaccurate, or too limited to show understanding.

items.copiedTextCapRule: A response made entirely or almost entirely of copied passage text with no student explanation earns no more than 1 point, even if the copied text is relevant.

scoreBandExamples.response: Nia changes because at first she tries to do every lantern job alone. The passage says she wrote twelve jobs beside her name, but later she crossed out half her jobs and added names beside them. Those details show she learns to share the work.
scoreBandExamples.why: States the change and explains two independent passage details.

scoreBandExamples.response: Nia changes by letting people help her. She crossed out half of her jobs and added names beside them.
scoreBandExamples.why: Explains the change but uses only one independent support.

scoreBandExamples.response: She crossed out half of her own jobs and added names beside them. Mateo tested the bulbs. Grandma taped the stars.
scoreBandExamples.why: Mostly copied details with little explanation.

scoreBandExamples.response: Nia wants a dog and goes to school late.

partialCreditRules.rule: Correct answer plus at least two accurate, independent passage details or quotes.
Partial answer with at least one accurate passage detail; minor inaccuracy or incomplete explanation may remain.
Incomplete answer, no useful text support, misunderstanding, or relevant copy-only response.
Insufficient, unrelated, or inaccurate response.

scoring.scoringNotes: Rubric metadata is validated for reviewer use only; this PR does not implement automated scoring.

items.subject: ELA
items.stem: How do Milo's actions show a change in what he thinks is important? Use two details from the passage to support your answer.
items.instructionText: Write a short answer that explains Milo's change and supports the answer with two passage details.
items.expectedAnswerCore: Milo changes from protecting a perfect blank page to caring more about helping Tessa and remembering that moment.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: Milo wanted to keep it clean until he could draw something perfect.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: Milo first wants the sketchbook page to stay clean and perfect.
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This shows what Milo values at the beginning.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: Then he saw Tessa chasing pins near the rose bushes, so he set the book down and ran to help.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: Milo sets down the book and helps Tessa.
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This shows he chooses helping over keeping the page untouched.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: Milo said the first page was special after all because it showed the day the porch bell reminded him to help.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: Milo decides the page is special because it shows the day he helped.
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This shows he values the helpful memory more than perfection.

rubric.points3: A 3-point response states that Milo changes from protecting a perfect blank page to caring more about helping Tessa and remembering that moment. It uses at least two accurate details from The Porch Bell.
rubric.points2: A 2-point response gives a partial explanation of Milo's change and includes at least one accurate passage detail from The Porch Bell.
rubric.points1: A 1-point response gives a weak or incomplete idea about Milo, gives no useful text support, or is mostly copied passage text from The Porch Bell.
rubric.points0: A 0-point response is unrelated to The Porch Bell, inaccurate, or too limited to show understanding.

items.copiedTextCapRule: A response made entirely or almost entirely of copied passage text with no student explanation earns no more than 1 point, even if the copied text is relevant.

scoreBandExamples.response: Milo changes because first he wants the sketchbook to stay clean so the first drawing can be perfect. Later he sets the book down and runs to help Tessa, and he says the page is special because it shows that day. These details show helping becomes more important than a perfect page.
scoreBandExamples.why: Explains the change and uses two independent passage details.

scoreBandExamples.response: Milo changes because he helps Tessa. The passage says he set the book down and ran to help her.
scoreBandExamples.why: Gives the change with one clear support but does not fully explain both sides.

scoreBandExamples.response: Milo wanted to keep it clean until he could draw something perfect. Then he saw Tessa chasing pins near the rose bushes.
scoreBandExamples.why: Mostly copied passage text with little explanation.

scoreBandExamples.response: Milo dislikes bells and throws away the sketchbook.

partialCreditRules.rule: Correct answer plus at least two accurate, independent passage details or quotes.
Partial answer with at least one accurate passage detail; minor inaccuracy or incomplete explanation may remain.
Incomplete answer, no useful text support, misunderstanding, or relevant copy-only response.
Insufficient, unrelated, or inaccurate response.
